title: You're Not Bad at Meditation. You're Doing the Wrong Step.
hook: Meditation isn't the thing you think it is. It's the step AFTER the thing you think it is. No wonder you're struggling.

script.visual: Person sitting cross-legged on a meditation cushion, eyes scrunched shut, brow furrowed. Timer shows 2:14. Cut to face.
script.voiceover: You sit down to meditate. Two minutes in, you're thinking about lunch. You think you failed. You didn't. You just named the wrong thing 'meditation.'
script.text_overlay: You didn't fail. You mislabeled the step.

script.visual: Three text cards slide across the screen: DHARANA — DHYANA — SAMADHI. The middle one pulses.
script.voiceover: Patanjali stacks three things. Dharana — concentration. Dhyana — meditation. Samadhi — absorption. Each one is built on the one before. And here is the joke: what you are calling 'meditation'... is almost always just dharana. And dharana is supposed to be hard.

script.visual: Visual metaphor: a person lifting weights, dropping them, picking them up again. Then intercut: another person sitting and continuously pulling attention back to their breath.
script.voiceover: Dharana is the rep. You pick a single object — the breath, a mantra, a candle — you focus, your mind runs, you pull it back. Focus. Run. Pull back. Focus. Run. Pull back. That is not a meditation fail. That is a dharana workout. Sutra 3.2 describes dhyana — actual meditation — as pratyaya-ekatanata. The unbroken flow. When you have done enough reps of pulling the mind back that it just... stays.

script.visual: Water pouring from a jug. First in chunky bursts — splash, splash, splash. Then, slowly, it becomes a continuous, silent stream.
script.voiceover: Patanjali's image for it: water poured from one vessel to another. At first it splashes out in chunks — that is dharana, your attention in dropouts. Eventually it becomes a single unbroken stream. No gaps. That unbroken stream — that is dhyana. You do not force it. You earn it. Through a lot of splashing.
script.text_overlay: Dharana is splashing. Dhyana is the stream.

script.visual: Person's meditation timer, now at 08:32. They look calm, not because the mind is quiet, but because they stopped expecting it to be.
script.voiceover: Stop grading yourself on dhyana. You are doing dharana. That is the actual work.
script.text_overlay: You're not bad at meditation. You're early.

script.voiceover: Next session — count the pull-backs instead of judging the wandering. Tell me the number.

cta: Next time you sit — count how many times you pull your mind back. Don't judge it. That number IS the practice. Drop your count.
caption:
You sit down to meditate. Two minutes in, you're thinking about a work email. You conclude: I'm bad at this.

You are not bad at this. You are confusing two different things.

Sutra 3.2: तत्र प्रत्ययैकतानता ध्यानम्
tatra pratyaya-ekatanata dhyanam

"Dhyana is the unbroken flow of cognition toward the object."

Patanjali stacks three stages:

1. DHARANA — concentration. Picking one object. Pulling the mind back every time it wanders. This is the rep.
2. DHYANA — meditation. The unbroken flow. When you have done enough pull-backs that the mind just... stays.
3. SAMADHI — absorption. The knower and the known dissolve into each other.

What you are calling 'meditation' is almost always dharana. And dharana is SUPPOSED to be hard. Every pull-back is the rep. If your mind wanders 73 times in 10 minutes, you did 73 reps. That is not failure. That is the workout.

Patanjali's image: water poured from one vessel to another. At first it splashes out in chunks. Eventually, a single unbroken stream. You earn the stream through the splashing.

Stop grading yourself on dhyana. You are doing dharana. That is the actual work.

#yogasutras #patanjali #dharana #dhyana #meditation #mindfulness #yogaphilosophy #consciousness #ancientwisdom #yogadarshan #meditationtips #focus #attention #samadhi #innerwork
music_suggestion: Intermittent, chunky piano notes that represent the 'splashes' of dharana. Gradually the gaps close until it becomes a continuous ambient drone — the stream — for the final 15 seconds.
